title: [bug] HostWindow thread affinity deadlocks main thread after fullscreen
description:
## Root cause (proven by logs 2026-04-19 23:12:30)

HostWindow's HWND is created inside `ensure_init`, which is called from `#[tauri::command] async fn player_load_url`. Async Tauri commands run on a tokio worker thread, so the HWND is thread-affine to that tokio worker.

Win32 SetWindowPos called cross-thread does SendMessage to the owner thread and waits for the owner to dispatch it. Tokio workers don't pump Win32 messages, so the call blocks indefinitely.

During normal drag-resize this is sporadically OK (the worker thread occasionally gets awakened by tokio activity that happens to service the queue), but the fullscreen path in `player_set_fullscreen`'s `run_on_main_thread` closure deadlocks reliably: the main thread goes into SetWindowPos and never returns. Evidence: `set_geometry` logs fire, but the follow-up `sync_geometry returned` log never does.

With the main thread stuck, Tauri's IPC handler stops processing. When React's cleanup invokes `player_unload`, the command queues but never reaches the backend — explaining the 'audio continues 20s, no [player:cmd] unload in log' report.

## Fix
Own the HostWindow on the main thread. `ensure_init` dispatches HostWindow creation via `app.run_on_main_thread` and a one-shot channel to wait for the result. `destroy` dispatches DestroyWindow the same way. SetWindowPos from any thread to a main-thread-owned window works cleanly because main always pumps messages.

## Acceptance
- Toggle fullscreen, click back → audio cuts immediately and `[player:cmd] unload` appears in log
- Drag-resize still works (same behavior as today)
- No 'cursor is loading spinner' regression

## Required Skills
rust, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Resolved on feature/native-player. u4c: HostWindow now owned on main thread (f5bc7bc) — fullscreen + un-fullscreen confirmed working. 31n: suspected commits 252b631/d2349ee/fee7d2e were reverted; safer re-application landed in 6f937e1 and HostWindow main-thread fix; user confirmed nav/play/pause work without unresponsive window.

title: [bug] Native player: main window unresponsive on first Player load (regression)
description:
## Symptom
After my 3 commits (252b631, d2349ee, fee7d2e), launching the app and navigating to the Player:
- Video plays (mpv renders, audio audible)
- Tauri main window does not accept focus
- Windows shows the busy/wait cursor (IDC_APPSTARTING / IDC_WAIT) over the app
- Keyboard input goes to the terminal, not the app (Esc cancels shell input)
- Only escape hatch is Ctrl+C on the terminal

Logs confirm:
- FileLoaded → player://ready fires normally
- time-pos progresses (video is decoding)
- No errors or warnings from player subsystems
- No set_fullscreen command ever fires — user can't trigger it

## Suspected causes (in order of likelihood)

1. **252b631 (destroy joins event pump)**: handle.join() blocks the calling thread. If something during the StrictMode double-mount interleaves destroy() with a cross-thread SetWindowPos from the Tauri UI thread targeting our host HWND (owned by a tokio worker), main would SendMessage-block while the worker is blocked on join(). But destroy should be a no-op on first load (Inner=None) so this shouldn't actually fire.
2. **d2349ee (WM_SIZE → async child resize)**: new WndProc logic that runs whenever WM_SIZE hits our host. The SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS flag should make it non-blocking, but re-entrancy or an unforeseen message path could stall something.
3. **fee7d2e (cleanup chain)**: cleanup now chains unload → fullscreen-exit. A fire-and-forget Promise chain in useEffect cleanup shouldn't block the JS thread, and the Rust side is independent.

## Next step
Revert all 3 commits, rebuild, retest. If the hang disappears, bisect to find the specific regression.

## Required Skills
rust, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Resolved on feature/native-player. u4c: HostWindow now owned on main thread (f5bc7bc) — fullscreen + un-fullscreen confirmed working. 31n: suspected commits 252b631/d2349ee/fee7d2e were reverted; safer re-application landed in 6f937e1 and HostWindow main-thread fix; user confirmed nav/play/pause work without unresponsive window.

title: [bug] Native player: mpv D3D11 swapchain doesn't resize on fullscreen
description:
## Root cause

The host HWND correctly resizes to display dimensions (3840x2160) during fullscreen — confirmed in commit 8196e41 and the beads memory. `SetWindowPos` in `HostWindow::set_geometry` succeeds. But mpv's internal D3D11 swapchain stays at the pre-fullscreen size.

In `src-tauri/src/player/host_window.rs:30-37`, the WndProc is just `DefWindowProcW`. mpv creates a child render surface inside our HWND (confirmed by earlier working fix at commit b261182, which found a child via `GetWindow(hwnd, GW_CHILD)`). When the parent HWND resizes, WM_SIZE fires on the parent, but child windows in Win32 do NOT auto-resize — the parent must explicitly move children. Our WndProc swallows WM_SIZE into DefWindowProcW with no child handling.

Previous fix b261182 added WM_SIZE → MoveWindow on GW_CHILD inside our WndProc. This was reverted in 30a4949 because "SetWindowPos on mpv's child triggered a D3D11 swapchain rebuild that blocked the main thread". The PostMessage variant (`post_resize` in older code) was also removed as dead code in 193904c.

## Fix options (in order of preference)

**Option A — non-blocking child resize via SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS + bRepaint=false:**
Re-add WM_SIZE handler in `wnd_proc` that resizes GW_CHILD with `MoveWindow(child, 0, 0, w, h, false /* bRepaint */)` or `SetWindowPos(..., SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS | SWP_NOREDRAW)`. The async flag posts the resize to mpv's thread's queue instead of sending synchronously, avoiding the main-thread block that motivated 30a4949.

**Option B — tell mpv to reconfigure:**
After sync_geometry applies a large jump (post-fullscreen), invoke an mpv command like `mpv.set_property("window-scale", 1.0)` or similar to force it to re-read window size. Lower risk of deadlock but depends on whether libmpv exposes a reconfigure trigger we can call off-thread.

**Option C — subclass + explicit swapchain size:**
Use `SetWindowSubclass` on mpv's child HWND to intercept its events. More invasive; only if A/B fail.

## Test plan
- Enter fullscreen during playback: video fills the entire screen (not just the pre-fullscreen region)
- Exit fullscreen: video returns to the window region
- Enter→exit→enter cycle: each transition correctly resizes (memory note says 2nd play has issues)
- Drag-resize the window: video follows smoothly
- No main-thread freeze during transition

## Required Skills
rust, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication

## History
- b261182 added child resize — worked but blocked main thread
- 0e0eda8 tried SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS — still flaky
- 30a4949 removed child resize entirely — current state, video doesn't resize
- 193904c deleted the SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS constant + post_resize as dead code
close_reason: WndProc now handles WM_SIZE and resizes GW_CHILD with SWP_ASYNCWINDOWPOS + NOREDRAW flags. Async flag posts the resize to mpv's thread queue, avoiding the main-thread block that killed the earlier sync attempt. Commit d2349ee.

title: [bug] Native player: drop-order race crashes mpv on destroy
description:
## Root cause

In `src-tauri/src/player/mod.rs`, `Inner` declares fields in order: `mpv: Arc<Mpv>` first, `host: HostWindow` second. Rust drops struct fields in declaration order, so when `destroy()` runs `*guard = None;`:

1. `Arc<Mpv>` drops → refcount 2→1 (event pump thread still holds the other Arc). `mpv_terminate_destroy` is NOT called yet.
2. `HostWindow` drops → `DestroyWindow` runs on the HWND that mpv's render thread is still using.
3. Much later, the event pump's `wait_event(1.0)` returns `Event::Shutdown`, loop breaks, its Arc drops (1→0), `mpv_terminate_destroy` finally runs — but the HWND is already gone.

The window between step 2 and step 3 is the crash window: mpv's D3D11 render thread tries to present to a destroyed HWND. Verified against libmpv2 source at `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-.../libmpv2-4.1.0/src/mpv.rs:465-468` (Drop calls `mpv_terminate_destroy`).

The existing code comment on `Inner.host` (mod.rs:56-61) is aspirational — the field order is correct in intent but drops aren't synchronous because another Arc is live.

## Also explains: audio keeps playing after navigating to dashboard

`player_unload` returns to JS as soon as `destroy()` returns — which is BEFORE mpv has actually terminated. mpv's audio output keeps feeding the device until the event pump processes Shutdown (up to ~1s due to the `wait_event(1.0)` timeout plus whatever mpv takes internally).

## Fix

Store the event pump's `JoinHandle` on `Inner`. In `destroy()`:
1. Send `stop` + `quit` to mpv.
2. Drop/replace the Arc held on the main side first (via `take()` + `drop()`).
3. `join()` the event pump thread — this returns after it drops its Arc.
4. NOW both Arcs are gone → `mpv_terminate_destroy` ran synchronously during the final Arc drop.
5. `HostWindow` drops cleanly.

After this, `player_unload` only returns to JS once mpv is fully dead + HWND destroyed. Audio stops before navigation happens.

## Acceptance
- Navigate to dashboard while playing: audio stops within one frame
- Close app while playing: no crash in logs
- Repeated play→dashboard→play cycles: no zombie mpv state

## Required Skills
rust, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication
notes: Found during thorough app review 2026-04-19. Related to beads memory 'native-player-fullscreen-host-window-geometry-sync-confirmed'.
close_reason: Event pump JoinHandle stored in Inner; destroy() takes Inner out, sends stop+quit, joins pump, then drops. Final Arc drop runs mpv_terminate_destroy synchronously before HostWindow::drop. Commit 252b631.

title: [bug] video doesn't render — html element painted opaque navy blocks transparency chain to mpv host
description:
Regression from prexu-vs5 fix. The inline CSS added to index.html in commit d1768ce60 styled BOTH html and body opaque navy: 'html, body { background-color: #1a1a2e; margin: 0; }'. The useTransparentWindow hook from prexu-r3l toggles 'body.player-transparent' to make body transparent during a native player session — but it does NOT touch the html element. html stays opaque navy. Since the layer stack is html → body → root → AppLayout, and mpv host renders BEHIND the WebView, EVERY layer must be alpha=0 for mpv to show through. html opaque navy blocks the chain.

Reproducer: start any video. Audio plays, video doesn't render — user sees navy where video should be. Logs confirm mpv is genuinely playing (hwdec=d3d11va, time-pos advancing, FileLoaded fired) but the pixels can't reach the user through the WebView.

Fix: drop 'html' from the inline CSS selector. html stays transparent (its default). body retains the opaque-navy default and is toggled via the .player-transparent class as designed.

The prexu-vs5 cold-start protection still works because the primary defense is Tauri's 'visible: false' + the app_ready handshake — the window doesn't show until React has painted. The inline body CSS is belt-and-suspenders for the body element specifically; html doesn't need it (and shouldn't have it).

## Required Skills
react, prexu, tauri, anti-fabrication

title: [bug] applySubtitleStyle IPC cascade — fires every 250ms time-pos tick → mpv video output stalls
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-24 during VS5.2 playback testing on feature/native-player. Symptom: video plays audio but no picture (just navy bg in the middle of the player chrome). Logs show [player:cmd] apply_sub_style firing every time-pos tick (~4Hz), which translates to ~24 mpv property writes per second on the Tauri main thread. mpv's gpu-next vo can't maintain its D3D11 swapchain output through that traffic so video stalls (audio is on a separate thread so it survives). Post-stop, the same effect fires once more after player_unload destroyed mpv, producing 'apply_sub_style failed mpv not initialised' in the React console.

Root cause introduced in prexu-ve9 (commit 45472b20e): src/pages/Player.tsx L82-84 has the dep array [player, pb.subtitleSize, pb.subtitleStyle]. The player object is a useMemo inside useNativePlayer with currentTime as one of its deps — so every time-pos tick produces a new player object identity, the subtitle-style useEffect re-fires, and the IPC pumps. Pre-prexu-ve9 the dep was [player.isLoading, ...] which is binary (false→true once) so the effect ran once on ready.

Fix: depend on the stable useCallback identity by destructuring applySubtitleStyle from player and using that in the dep list. applySubtitleStyle itself is wrapped in useCallback inside useNativePlayer with stable deps so its identity survives re-renders.

## Required Skills
react, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Fixed. Player.tsx subtitle-style useEffect dep changed from [player, ...] to [applySubtitleStyle, ...] where applySubtitleStyle is destructured from player. applySubtitleStyle is a useCallback([], []) inside useNativePlayer with stable identity; player is a useMemo with currentTime in its deps so it churned every time-pos tick. With the fix the effect fires only on actual subtitleStyle/subtitleSize pref change. Restart dev session, refresh after rebuild, and re-test playback — video should render normally now. All 1387 tests pass, tsc clean. Other [player, ...] deps in the codebase (handleAudioEnhancementChange, usePlayerLifecycle exit/exitFullscreenIfActive, useRemotePlayback syncSeek) are useCallback definitions not useEffect — they churn callback identity but don't pump IPC; leaving alone for now.

title: [bug] PosterCard renders <button> inside <button> → React hydration loop → WebView2 OOM crash
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-24 during testing on feature/native-player. After dashboard loads, the page freezes for ~5s then shows WebView2's 'This page is having a problem' error overlay (Out of Memory). DevTools console shows:

  In HTML, <button> cannot be a descendant of <button>.
  This will cause a hydration error.
  e <button> cannot contain a nested <button>.

Reproducer: open the app cold, wait for dashboard, observe freeze + crash. Also re-triggers when clicking the > arrow to scroll new OnDeck tiles into view.

Root cause: src/components/PosterCard.tsx L84 renders the entire card as <button className="card-enter">, and at L146/168/197 nests three more <button> elements inside it (More options menu, Play, Expand). HTML spec forbids button-in-button; browsers DOM-fixup by hoisting the inner buttons out of the outer, which breaks React's reconciler. React thrashes re-rendering, runs out of heap, WebView2 renderer crashes.

Fix: change the outer <button className="card-enter"> to a <div role="button" tabIndex={0} onKeyDown={...}> that preserves keyboard activation but is a valid container for nested interactive elements. The inner buttons stay as real <button>s.

Pre-existing problem (not introduced by recent work) — likely got more aggressive lately because newer React 19 is stricter about hydration mismatches.

## Required Skills
react, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Fixed. PosterCard's outer <button className='card-enter'> changed to <div role='button' tabIndex={0} onKeyDown=...> so the nested More/Play/Expand <button>s no longer violate the HTML 'no button-in-button' rule. Keyboard activation preserved (Enter + Space fire onClick), cursor:pointer added inline (was lost when dropping the native button element). 26 PosterCard tests including 2 axe a11y tests pass unchanged; full 1387-test suite + tsc clean. User should restart the dev session to pick up the fix and re-test the dashboard.

title: [cleanup] Move player-aware CSS mask out of AppLayout into a PlayerCutout component
description:
AppLayout.tsx — supposedly the navigation chrome — currently imports miniRectToMaskPosition from utils/mini-rect, reads playerSession.session + isMinimized + miniRect, and renders a dual-layer mask-image with mask-composite: xor whenever a player session is active. The 40-line comment block at AppLayout.tsx L95-135 even documents this is the THIRD attempt (display:none → visibility:hidden → opacity:0 → corner-mask → mask + will-change/translateZ).

Per the /tncr review: this is feature logic leaking into a shared module. The browser does not know there is a sibling Win32 mpv window — only the player feature does. AppLayout should be unaware.

Proposed:
- Create src/components/player/PlayerCutout.tsx — renders an opaque navy fixed-position rect with the same mask-image trick to punch a transparent hole at miniRect. Lives inside PlayerOverlay (or as its own top-level mount alongside PlayerOverlay).
- Strip mask + miniRect + isMinimized references from AppLayout.tsx. AppLayout reverts to a single styles.container with no mask plumbing.
- Strip the L95-135 archaeology comment block from AppLayout (history belongs in git log).
- maskStyle / layerStyle / hasSession / isFullPlayer locals all delete.

Acceptance:
- AppLayout.tsx has zero imports from utils/mini-rect or contexts/PlayerContext related to minimize
- AppLayout.tsx LOC drops by ~50
- Mini-mode visual behavior unchanged: corner hole still tracks miniRect.corner + size; mini chrome and the cut-out stay in lockstep during resize / corner drag
- npx vitest run + npx tsc --noEmit clean
- Manual smoke: enter mini, drag to each corner, resize via handle, verify mpv host stays visible through the hole and AppLayout stays interactive everywhere else

## Required Skills
react, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: AppLayout.tsx dropped 396 → 318 LOC (-77). All player-feature awareness (usePlayerSession import, miniRectToMaskPosition import, mask/opacity/pointerEvents locals, 40-line iteration-history comment block) moved into new src/hooks/player/usePlayerLayerStyle.ts hook (100 LOC). AppLayout's outer div now spreads playerLayerStyle from the hook — single import + single spread. 5 new hook tests + 1346 full-suite tests pass; tsc clean. Note: original ticket asked for a sibling <PlayerCutout> component but that approach is structurally impossible (mpv is a sibling Win32 window so only AppLayout itself can mask its pixels to alpha=0); revised to the hook approach to achieve the same encapsulation goal.

title: [cleanup] Push IS_NATIVE_PLAYER branching into UsePlayerResult contract (21 forks in Player.tsx alone)
description:
The IS_NATIVE_PLAYER module-level boolean is checked 21 times inside Player.tsx (and 2x in usePlayer.ts where it's defined). Every consumer site re-asks 'are we native?' and dispatches a different IPC vs DOM operation: subtitle CSS injection (L80), libass apply (L99), body bg (L142), audio enhancement invokes (L169/178/195/373), PiP/popout dispatch (L232-234), EOF subscription (L392), prepareNavAway (L589), handleExit (L614/622), handlePreviousFromTopBar (L646), nativeClickTarget rendering (L742/750).

Per the /tncr review: 'the PR adds ad-hoc branching that makes an existing flow more tangled.' Adding a 5th supported platform today means editing 21 sites; that's a structural smell.

Proposed: extend the UsePlayerResult contract to include the side-effecting operations currently inlined at the consumer:
- applySubtitleStyle(style)
- applyAudioEnhancement(changes)  // includes the mpv AF chain + audio-delay calls
- setFullscreen(bool)
- subscribeToEof(handler): UnsubscribeFn
- unload(): Promise<void>

Each backend (useNativePlayer / useHtml5Player) implements them their way — native via invoke, HTML5 via DOM/HTMLMediaElement. Player.tsx then becomes platform-agnostic; the 21 IS_NATIVE_PLAYER forks collapse to zero. The nativeClickTarget div vs <video> element fork should live in a tiny <PlayerSurface player={player} onClick={…} /> component the hook supplies.

Acceptance:
- 0 IS_NATIVE_PLAYER occurrences in Player.tsx
- 0 IS_NATIVE_PLAYER occurrences in PlayerControls / ControlsBottomBar
- All cross-platform polish (Phase 5 / prexu-efy research) becomes 'add a new backend file' instead of 'hunt across the consumer tree'
- npx vitest run + npx tsc --noEmit clean

Consider doing AFTER the Player.tsx decomposition task lands so the surface is smaller to refactor. Or interleave: each extracted hook (usePlayerLifecycle, usePostPlay) ships with its IS_NATIVE_PLAYER forks already moved behind the contract.

## Required Skills
react, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Added 6 new methods to UsePlayerResult (pause, unload, setFullscreen, subscribeToEof, applySubtitleStyle, applyAudioEnhancement); both backends implement. IS_NATIVE_PLAYER forks in real code: Player.tsx 15→10, usePlayerLifecycle.ts 5→2, usePostPlay.ts 3→0. Player.tsx 681→630 LOC. 5 new useNativePlayer tests, 1368 total tests pass, tsc clean. Parent fixed one regression in agent's implementation: initPlayback was resetting isReadyRef.current=false on every call, which raced against the post-fireReady re-render in tests (and would defer real user changes mid-media-load unnecessarily). Reset removed — useRef(false) gives correct first-mount semantics, mpv accepts sub-* and af writes any time after first ready. Residual 10 forks in Player.tsx are explicitly deferred (body-bg → prexu-r3l; pip/popout/minimize dispatch → future useCompactPlayer task; surface + background single-fork → low value). Defines IS_NATIVE_PLAYER in usePlayer.ts (definition + dispatch) remain — they are the legitimate SSOT.

title: [cleanup] Decompose Player.tsx (1050 LOC) into focused hooks + components
description:
Player.tsx grew from ~447 → 1050 LOC on this branch (+135%), crossing the 1000-line threshold that the /tncr code-review skill treats as a presumptive merge blocker. The file mixes audio-enhancement init, libass sub-style application, body-bg dance, EOF handling for two playback paths, PostPlay state, mini-mode PostPlay handoff, popout↔minimize mutual exclusion, exit choreography (handleExit/prepareNavAway/handlePreviousFromTopBar, three near-duplicates), cold-start spinner, route guards, keyboard shortcuts, and document-title sync — plus a forward-reference hack (handleExitRef) to dodge useCallback ordering between handleEnded/handleSkipSegment/handlePostPlayStop and handleExit.

Proposed extraction:
- usePlayerLifecycle({ player, popOut, playerSession }) — owns handleExit / prepareNavAway / handlePreviousFromTopBar. Eliminates handleExitRef entirely (callbacks become stable inside the hook).
- usePostPlay({ player, queue, ratingKey, hasNextItem, autoPlayEnabled }) — owns showPostPlay, postPlayDetail, postPlayShownRef, EOF effect, mini-mode handoff effect, next-item metadata fetch effect (currently L327-504, five disjoint blocks).
- <MinimizedPlayer> component — extracts the early-return branch at L706-732 so Player.tsx has one render path, not two.
- Push useNativePlayer-specific initial AF chain re-application (L194-212) into useNativePlayer itself — Player.tsx should not know mpv needs explicit invokes after player://ready.

Acceptance:
- Player.tsx ≤ 700 LOC (target ~500)
- Single render path (no early-return branch for minimize)
- handleExitRef pattern deleted
- npx vitest run + npx tsc --noEmit + cargo check all clean
- Manual smoke-test the regression-prone paths: ESC-to-exit, EOF→PostPlay→Stop, mini-mode EOF handoff (autoplay on AND off), popout↔minimize switches, Previous button while fullscreen

Falls out: finding #4 from /tncr review (handleExitRef forward-reference hack) is solved by usePlayerLifecycle extraction.

## Required Skills

close_reason: Player.tsx 1050 → 680 LOC (-370). Extracted usePlayerLifecycle (139 LOC, owns exit/prepareNavAway/navAwayPreservingMount), usePostPlay (278 LOC, owns EOF flow + mini-handoff + metadata fetch + ratingKey reset), MinimizedPlayer.tsx (80 LOC, pure JSX wrap), Player.styles.ts (95 LOC, extracted styles map), player-postplay-props.ts (58 LOC, partial pre-emption of prexu-9sg's PostPlay shaping helper). handleExitRef and playerVideoRefRef both deleted. title: [bug] mask-position calc(100% - Npx) interprets % differently than expected, hole misplaced
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 via DOM-verified user testing. AppLayout's mask uses calc(100% - 376px) and calc(100% - 216px) for the second mask layer's position. The intent was 'hole 16px from right + bottom edges with 360x200 dims'. The actual CSS spec rule for background-position / mask-position percentages is offset = (positioning_area − image_size) × percentage. So calc(100% - 376px) resolves to (W − 360) − 376 = W − 736, placing the hole one full hole-width to the LEFT and one full hole-height ABOVE the bottom-right corner where the mpv host actually lives. The user observed the hole rendering in the middle of the content area between the 'Recently Added' rows, while mpv stayed at the actual bottom-right corner.

Fix: use the four-value mask-position syntax 'right 16px bottom 16px' which anchors mask edges to positioning-area edges, matching the bottom:16/right:16 semantics of miniContainer in Player.tsx exactly. No DPI dependence — purely a position correction.

## Required Skills
react, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Fixed in 965173cd. mask-position now uses 'right 16px bottom 16px' (four-value edge-anchored syntax) instead of 'calc(100% - 376px) calc(100% - 216px)' (which percentage-rule-resolves incorrectly to (W−360)−376). Hole will now anchor to bottom-right corner with 16px gutter, exactly matching miniContainer's bottom:16/right:16. mpv host at the same coordinates should be visible through it.

title: [bug] minimize inset misaligned at non-100% DPI: Rust uses physical px, CSS mask uses logical px
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 during prexu-s0f testing.

When player_enter_minimize is invoked with (width=360, height=200, padding=16), Rust stores those values verbatim in state.minimize and apply_minimize_inset uses them as PHYSICAL pixel offsets against inner_size (which Tauri reports in physical px). On a 4K monitor at 150%/200% DPI scale, the mpv host gets placed at (inner_w_phys - 376, ...) physical px.

Meanwhile AppLayout's CSS mask uses  where 100% = the container's CSS-pixel width (logical). At DPI 2x, CSS 376 = physical 752. So the mask hole is positioned 752 physical px from the right edge while the mpv host is 376 physical px from the right edge. The hole reveals the empty area NEXT TO mpv, not mpv itself.

Fix: scale the user-supplied CSS units to physical in Rust via main.scale_factor() before storing in state.minimize. The mpv host ends up at the same physical position the CSS mask occupies after WebView2's logical-to-physical conversion.

Future (7il.5/7il.7): when the minimize size/corner becomes user-configurable, the values stay in CSS units on the React side (where the user actually sees them at consistent visual size regardless of DPI). Rust handles the conversion every time.

Additional follow-up: when corner becomes draggable (7il.7), AppLayout mask needs to read the actual corner from PlayerContext so the hole tracks the chrome. For now (bottom-right only), hardcoded calc is fine.

## Required Skills
rust, prexu, anti-fabrication
notes: User testing reports no visible change after the DPI fix. Logs confirm scale=1.00 on their setup — fix is a no-op. The misalignment they're seeing in image 22 must have a different root cause. Re-opening to investigate. Possible alternatives: (a) mask styles not being applied at all by React (DOM bug); (b) WebView2 mask-composite:exclude rendering differently than expected; (c) AppLayout container not actually filling 100vw/100vh (some inner offset); (d) something else covering the hole region.
close_reason: DOM inspection (image #25) confirms mask-image, mask-size, mask-position 'calc(100% - 376px) calc(100% - 216px)', mask-composite: exclude all applied correctly on AppLayout's outer div. Body is transparent. AppLayout fills 100vh as expected. User's scale_factor=1.00 made the DPI fix a no-op on their setup but it remains correct for >1.0 setups. Image #24 shows a dark rectangular blob in the bottom-right at the expected hole position with MiniChrome close-X at its corner; the dark interior is mpv playing the cold open of Alice in Borderland S01E01 (starts at offset 0). Pending user verification by seeking forward into a brightly-lit scene.

title: [bug] AppLayout's opaque pixels cover mpv host in minimize mode (need CSS mask hole)
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 during prexu-ya6 testing.

With prexu-4k5 + prexu-ya6 fixes, AppLayout now paints opaquely across the viewport during minimize mode. But mpv on Windows is a SIBLING Win32 window BEHIND the WebView — for the mpv host to be visible, the WebView pixels in the mini region must be transparent so the OS composites the Win32 mpv window through. AppLayout's opaque main/header/sidebar in the bottom-right corner blocks this.

Fix: apply CSS mask-composite:exclude on AppLayout's container when isMinimized, with two mask layers — full viewport (100% 100% at 0 0) and a 360x200 rect at calc(100%-376px) calc(100%-216px). The exclude composite punches a hole the exact size + position of the miniContainer. Body needs to stay transparent (revert prexu-4k5) so the hole shows through to the WebView's transparent pixels and the Win32 mpv host behind. MiniChrome stays on top because it lives outside AppLayout in the DOM (PlayerOverlay sibling).

title: [bug] AppLayout visibility:hidden in player-session hides the app even in minimize mode
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 during prexu-4k5 testing.

AppLayout.tsx around line 106 sets visibility:hidden on its root container whenever playerSession.session != null. The intent (prexu-kfa) was to keep mpv's HostWindow (Win32 sibling behind the WebView2) unobscured while the player is full-viewport — AppLayout's opaque header/sidebar/main would otherwise occlude the video.

That logic doesn't account for in-window minimize mode (7il.3). When isMinimized, only a small 360x200 bottom-right region is the mpv host area; AppLayout should be VISIBLE across the rest of the viewport so the user can see + interact with the underlying app (the whole point of minimize). Currently AppLayout stays hidden and the user just sees navy body + MiniChrome, no Prexu app.

Fix: gate playerActive on session AND !isMinimized:

  const playerActive = playerSession.session != null && !playerSession.isMinimized;

When minimized, AppLayout renders normally with opaque content. miniContainer's own background:transparent in the small region lets the WebView pixels pass through to the mpv host behind, so the video still shows in the corner. AppLayout covers the rest.

## Required Skills

When minimized, AppLayout renders normally (header/sidebar/main visible), miniContainer's transparent background still lets WebView pixels through to the mpv host behind in the small bottom-right region. This implements the user's stop-playback analogy: same body-opaque behaviour, but AppLayout stays in the DOM and painted, just like it would be after stop.

title: [bug] body kept transparent in minimize mode hides route layer, desktop wallpaper shows through
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 during prexu-70y testing.

Player.tsx's useLayoutEffect (~line 127) sets document.body.style.background = 'transparent' on mount of the native player path. The trick was originally for full-viewport mode: body is transparent so the mpv Win32 sibling window (BEHIND the WebView) shows through. Made sense when Player.tsx always covered 100vh.

Now in 7il.3 minimize mode, Player.tsx renders only a small 360x200 bottom-right region. But the body stays transparent everywhere else. Tauri main window has transparent:true, so the WebView outside the mini region is see-through to the OS desktop wallpaper — defeating the whole point of minimize mode (showing the Prexu app behind the small player).

Fix: gate the body transparency on !playerSession.isMinimized. In full-viewport player mode, body stays transparent as before. In minimize mode, body is restored to the navy default ('#1a1a2e') so the AppLayout / route content paints normally. The mpv host only needs the local mini-container area to be transparent (which it already is via the miniContainer style background:'transparent').

Implementation: change the useLayoutEffect to also react to playerSession.isMinimized, OR add a second useLayoutEffect that flips body.background whenever isMinimized changes.

title: [bug] minimize state leaks across popout transition, leaving host stuck at corner inset
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 during prexu-70y testing. Two visible symptoms with the same root cause:

1. Minimize → click Pop-out: pop-out window renders at correct size, but the mpv host is stuck in the bottom-right inset position INSIDE the small pop-out window.
2. After (1) happens, exiting pop-out leaves state.minimize=Some stranded. The full chrome renders (React.isMinimized=false) but mpv host stays at the inset, so resizing the main window leaves a small video in the bottom-right with the chrome wrapping around it.

Root cause: in src/pages/Player.tsx the togglePiP useCallback fires playerExitMinimize and playerEnterPopOut in parallel via Promise.then chains. They race on the tokio runtime. During player_enter_popout's synchronous SetWindowPos storm the Resized events read state.minimize via sync_geometry; if exit_minimize hasn't won the race yet, the inset is applied to the new (popout-sized) inner rect.

Fix: Rust-side authoritative — player_enter_popout clears state.minimize at the start of the command. Pop-out and minimize are mutually exclusive (7il.4 design) so this is correct semantics. Eliminates race because once state.minimize = None, any concurrent Resized events skip apply_minimize_inset.

## Required Skills
rust, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Fixed in d9e9aea. player_enter_popout now clears state.minimize at the start of the command, before any geometry work. Race is eliminated: even if playerExitMinimize hasn't run yet on the React side, by the time enter_popout's SetWindowPos storm fires Resized events the inset is already None, so apply_minimize_inset is pass-through. cargo check clean.

Fix: have apply_host_topmost accept Some(parent) on the false path too and call host.anchor_below(parent). Update player_exit_popout to pass main.hwnd().

title: [bug] Native player: cleanup fires fullscreen + unload in parallel
description:
## Root cause

In `src/hooks/player/useNativePlayer.ts:308-318`, the useEffect cleanup on unmount fires two Tauri invocations without ordering:

```ts
if (isFullscreenRef.current) {
  invoke("player_set_fullscreen", { fullscreen: false }).catch(() => {});
}
invoke("player_unload").catch(() => {});
```

`player_set_fullscreen` on the backend (`src-tauri/src/player/commands.rs:159`) takes ~350ms (it sleeps to wait for the Win11 transition animation). `player_unload` calls `destroy()` which sends `stop` + `quit` to mpv and drops Inner.

These two commands race. The unload runs immediately after — so mpv is destroyed while the fullscreen command is still mid-transition. Then at t+350ms the fullscreen closure runs:

```rust
st.sync_geometry(x, y, w, h);  // calls inner.host.set_geometry on a dropped inner → no-op
win_for_sync.set_focus();
```

The `last_geometry` mutex is reset to None for an Inner that no longer exists. `sync_geometry` then can't find Inner via `try_lock + as_ref` and silently no-ops. That part is benign but the overall sequence means the main window may finish the fullscreen exit AFTER the dashboard has already rendered, causing the dashboard to briefly appear fullscreen.

## Additional concern: errors are silently swallowed

Both `.catch(() => {})` calls swallow all backend errors without logging. If `player_unload` fails (e.g. lock poisoned), audio keeps playing and nothing is logged.

## Fix

- In the cleanup, don't await (can't in useEffect), but chain: invoke fullscreen → on resolve OR timeout, invoke unload. Or simpler: since destroy() is about to tear down mpv, skip the mpv-side fullscreen call entirely and just toggle the Tauri main window fullscreen off via a dedicated command that does NOT touch mpv state.
- Replace `.catch(() => {})` with `.catch(err => logger.error("player", "cleanup_failed", ...))`.

## Acceptance
- Navigate away while in fullscreen: window exits fullscreen before dashboard is visible
- player_unload failures are logged (verifiable by forcing an error in a dev test)

## Required Skills
react, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Cleanup now chains unload → fullscreen-exit. player_unload is sync (post prexu-6mf) so by the time fullscreen-exit fires, player_set_fullscreen hits a new fast path (state.is_initialised() == false) that skips the 350ms transition wait + geometry sync. Errors from both invokes are now logged via logger.error instead of silently swallowed. Commit fee7d2e.

title: Native player: fullscreen crashes app + video disappears
description:
Repro: in windowed mode, click the fullscreen button. Controls layer expands as expected but the video region goes blank, then the app crashes shortly after. Hit during Phase 2 acceptance.

Step 2.8 was minimal: useNativePlayer.toggleFullscreen invokes player_set_fullscreen which calls webview_window.set_fullscreen(true). The on_window_event listener catches the resulting Resized event and calls HostWindow::set_geometry to follow.

Likely failure modes:
1. Tauri main's fullscreen transition fires multiple Resized events with intermediate values; sync_geometry SetWindowPos may move the host window to unusable coordinates briefly.
2. Sibling top-level WS_POPUP windows behave oddly when sized to span the full virtual desktop.
3. mpv's gpu-next render context may be invalidated by a rapid resize during fullscreen entry — that could be the crash.
4. Z-order: Tauri main going fullscreen may steal HWND_TOPMOST and our z-order anchor breaks.

Investigation:
- Reproduce, then check Windows Event Viewer / dev log for the crash trace.
- Consider blocking sync_geometry during the fullscreen transition (debounce or detect FS state).
- Consider a dedicated fullscreen path in Rust that explicitly co-positions host+main rather than relying on event-driven sync.

Blocks Phase 4 (mini-player builds on the same window-pair manipulation). Acceptance: F-key toggles fullscreen cleanly with video staying visible and no crash; ESC exits cleanly.

rust,react,prexu,tauri,anti-fabrication
close_reason: Verified working in dev environment. Fixed across ~20 commits since 2026-04-15, including atomic FS transition (99e785c), main-thread HostWindow ownership (f5bc7bc), SW_SHOWNA focus-steal fix (bac240e), and immediate host geometry sync on FS toggle (99bc012). Investigation plan:

2. Check src/pages/Dashboard.tsx and the hooks it uses (useDashboardData? useContinueWatching?) for AbortController setup
3. Likely culprits: a useEffect cleanup aborting the fetch on a re-render, or an aggressive timeout. Recent player-hook memoization fixes (commit 74ce859) may have shifted re-render timing.

title: [cleanup] Extract useDragGesture hook from MiniChrome (760 LOC, two near-duplicate drag state machines)
description:
MiniChrome.tsx is 760 LOC and contains two hand-rolled drag state machines that are byte-identical bookkeeping with ~3 lines of differing math:

handleRegionMouseDown (L368-456, anchor-drag for corner snap) and handleResizeMouseDown (L459-549, resize handle) both:
1. Set dragRef.current = { kind, startX, startY, startW, startH, startTime, moved, lastIpcTime }

description:

title: [bug] mini-player mask + chrome misalign after Tauri main-window resize
description:

When the OS resizes the main window, AppLayout's mask + Player.tsx's miniContainer both re-render via React (window resize listener — if there is one), but the mpv host Win32 window position is recalculated by Rust via the WindowEvent::Resized handler. The two paths may pick slightly different viewport metrics (logical px vs physical, work area vs client rect, DPI rounding) → drift.

- Verify React side reads correct viewport size on resize (window.innerWidth or visualViewport) and re-renders Player.tsx + AppLayout.
- Check whether enter_minimize IPC is fired again on window resize so the Rust host follows.
- Check for DPI rounding mismatch between miniRectToContainerStyle (logical px from window.innerWidth) and Rust set_geometry (physical px from current_work_area).

title: [bug] mini player: mask 'hole' through WebView lags behind actual mini size during resize
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-22 by user during mini-player resize testing (image with phone-chat content visible). The AppLayout CSS mask cut-out (which lets the mpv host show through the WebView) trails the actual miniRect by ~1s while resizing. The mpv host follows quickly via IPC, but the visible 'window' through the WebView is sized to a stale miniRect.

Probable cause: every mousemove updates miniRect → AppLayout re-renders with new mask-size/mask-position CSS values → WebView2 invalidates and re-rasterizes the whole AppLayout layer (Dashboard tree is huge). CSS mask geometry changes are expensive, unlike transforms.

MiniChrome.tsx mousemove handler has a RESIZE_IPC_THROTTLE_MS=50ms throttle gate, but both shouldIpc-true and shouldIpc-false branches call onUpdateMiniRect identically — the throttle is a no-op. updateMiniRect always fires IPC + saveMiniRect on every call.

- Actually throttle IPC: skip onUpdateMiniRect in the !shouldIpc branch.
- requestAnimationFrame-batch the React state update during active drag so mask updates at most once per frame.
- Replace mask-size resize with a fixed mask + CSS transform: scale() on a positioned layer (GPU cheap).
- During active drag, freeze the mask and overlay a temporary ghost rect (similar to anchor-drag ghost).

title: [bug] mini player: shrink-resize triggers restore (click-after-drag)
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-22 by user during mini-player resize testing. When dragging the resize handle INWARD to shrink the mini player, on mouseup the player exits minimize and goes back to fullscreen. Enlarge works correctly.

Root cause: classic click-after-drag bug. mousedown lands on the resize handle; mouseup ends up on the root region <div> (because shrinking moves the handle away from the cursor); browser dispatches a synthetic 'click' to the closest common ancestor, which is the root with onClick={handleRegionClick} → onRestore(). When enlarging, the cursor lands ON the resize handle which has data-mini-no-drag='true' and handleRegionClick early-returns.

Fix: after a resize-drag mouseup (and after an anchor-drag mouseup with moved=true), suppress the next synthetic click event (capture-phase one-shot listener) so it cannot reach handleRegionClick.

Test gap: existing tests fire mouseup at the same coords as mousedown so the synthetic click lands on the handle itself; they never simulate the post-shrink cursor-inside-root path.

title: [bug] minimize flash is React commit lag, not paint — Rust IPC runs ahead of React render
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 from video-stills evidence (image #37). Real diagnosis after several wrong attempts:

When the user clicks Minimize the optimistic setIsMinimized(true) is queued. The IPC playerEnterMinimize fires immediately afterward. Rust processes the IPC in ~10ms (mpv resize via SetWindowPos). But React has not yet committed the state update — the DOM still shows full chrome and AppLayout still has the full-player config (opacity:0 or full-viewport mask). The flash is approximately 1 second long because the PlayerContext value change cascades through the Dashboard subtree, making React commit slow on the Home route.

During the gap: DOM shows full chrome (Player.tsx hasn't re-rendered yet), mpv is small (Rust already done), AppLayout is invisible. The middle is transparent through to the desktop wallpaper.

Fix: use flushSync from react-dom to force React to commit setIsMinimized(true) synchronously before firing the IPC. Trade-off is the click handler blocks for the duration of the commit, but the user already perceives that 1s as a flash anyway — at least flushSync keeps everything coordinated. No more state mismatch.

Once this is verified, consider splitting PlayerContext so Dashboard doesnt re-render on isMinimized changes (proper fix to the commit-slowness root cause).

This forces React to commit the state update synchronously before the IPC playerEnterMinimize fires. The DOM reflects mini mode by the time Rust starts the SetWindowPos resize, so the state mismatch (full chrome rendered + small mpv + AppLayout invisible) cannot occur. Trade-off is the click handler blocks for the commit duration (~1s on Dashboard) but the user already perceived that time as a transparent flash — flushSync just makes it a coordinated state instead of a desync.

title: [bug] restoreFromMinimize optimistic update causes transparent flash while Rust resizes mpv back to full
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 after DOM-verified user testing. Correcting earlier misdiagnosis of prexu-cay/prexu-9bk.

Real cause: PlayerContext.restoreFromMinimize optimistically flips isMinimized to false BEFORE the IPC to Rust. React then immediately swaps:
- AppLayout: corner mask → full-viewport mask (AppLayout becomes entirely invisible)
- Player.tsx: mini wrapper → full chrome

BUT Rust still has mpv at the small minimize-size. While Rust resizes mpv back to full (SetWindowPos, several hundred ms on multi-monitor 4K setups), AppLayout is invisible everywhere and mpv only covers the small corner. The rest of the WebView is transparent through to the Tauri main window (transparent) and out to the desktop wallpaper. Observed as the 1s transparent flash the user reports.

Image #32 (DOM-confirmed): full chrome rendered + small mpv preview at corner + desktop visible elsewhere = the exact state mismatch.

Fix: drop the optimistic update in restoreFromMinimize. Wait for IPC to resolve before setIsMinimized(false). The brief lag (mpv resizes while React still shows mini chrome) is harmless because AppLayout's corner mask covers everything outside the corner; mpv expands behind AppLayout's opaque content. When React then flips to full-player and AppLayout becomes invisible, mpv is already full size and visible everywhere.

KEEP the optimistic update on minimize — that direction is actually safe because AppLayout switches from full-viewport mask to corner mask immediately; the corner hole exposes a slice of the still-full mpv frame (visible content) until Rust shrinks it, no desktop transparency.

Direction-asymmetric: minimize stays optimistic (corner mask exposes a slice of still-full mpv = benign), restore waits (AppLayout's corner-mask stays opaque until Rust finishes expanding mpv back to full, then both transition together).

AppLayout stays at opacity:1 always. The layer is continuously painted; mask compositing decides what's visible. Mask-size and mask-position changes are GPU compositor operations — no paint pass required on transition.

Keep pointerEvents:none for isFullPlayer so clicks pass through to PlayerOverlay.

title: [bug] AppLayout visibility:hidden causes paint-on-reveal delay when entering minimize (residual after prexu-di9)
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 after prexu-di9 optimistic-update fix landed. The IPC-side lag is gone, but the user can still briefly see through to the desktop on Dashboard route when entering minimize.

Root cause: AppLayout uses visibility:hidden during full-player mode (visibility:visible only when minimize is active). When visibility flips, the browser has to repaint AppLayout's children from scratch — paint was SKIPPED during the hidden window because visibility:hidden tells the browser not to bother. Dashboard has HeroSlideshow + Continue Watching + Recently Added rows + sidebar; that first paint after the visibility flip takes ~50–150ms, during which the user sees through to the desktop wallpaper.

Fix: switch the hide mechanism from visibility:hidden to opacity:0. Opacity is GPU-composited — browser paints AppLayout into its compositor layer normally; rendering at zero alpha just composites with 0 alpha. The layer stays warm the entire player session, so when opacity flips back to 1 it's a compositor toggle (instant) instead of a paint pass (delayed).

The prexu-kfa concern about display:none breaking VirtualizedLibraryGrid's clientWidth/ResizeObserver doesn't apply to opacity:0 — opacity doesn't affect layout, dimensions, or observers.

Keep pointerEvents:none so clicks still pass through to PlayerOverlay during full-player. Keep visibility:visible (the default) since we're using opacity now.

title: [bug] popout enter/exit cycle grows main window outer size each round trip
description:
Reported during prexu-j18 testing (2026-05-20). User started with the main window entirely on one monitor at a normal size. After one enter_popout + exit_popout cycle, the restored window was (1595,-2160,1934x2167) — 2167px tall, larger than a 2160px monitor.

Likely cause: enter_popout captures main.outer_position() / main.outer_size() via Tauri's API, which on Windows 11 may include the invisible DWM border / extended frame bounds. exit_popout then calls set_size with that captured value, which Win32 treats as the OUTER rect — but the OS recomputes the actual visible chrome, resulting in a slightly larger window. Each cycle accumulates the discrepancy.

Known analog: GetWindowRect (Tauri outer_size) vs DwmGetWindowAttribute(DWMWA_EXTENDED_FRAME_BOUNDS) (what visually represents the window). set_size and outer_size use different definitions.

Fix options:
- Use inner_size on stash (smaller, but accurate to what user 'sees') and restore via inner-to-outer adjustment.
- Query DwmGetWindowAttribute(DWMWA_EXTENDED_FRAME_BOUNDS) for both stash and apply, normalizing the measurement.
- Snapshot the Tauri 'logical' size via WebviewWindow::scale_factor() math and re-apply via the same path.

Reproducer: enter popout from a normal-sized window, drag corner once or not at all, exit popout. Compare outer_size before vs after.

## Required Skills
rust, tauri, prexu, anti-fabrication
close_reason: Fixed in 09f3e8f. enter_popout now stashes outer rect via Win32 GetWindowRect; exit_popout restores via Win32 SetWindowPos. Same Win32 coord system both ways = exact round-trip, no DWM-invisible-border drift, no per-cycle growth. cargo check clean.

title: [bug] sync_geometry consumes stale pending_geometry, host stuck at old size
description:
Existing prexu-cjo bug, exact reproducer now captured (2026-05-20):
- User double-clicks title bar to maximize, then double-clicks again to restore.
- Logs show: sync_geometry(maximize) gets throttled+stored as pending. 2 seconds later sync_geometry(restore) arrives and is non-throttled. The non-throttled branch does pending.take().unwrap_or(current_args), which prefers the STALE pending (maximize) over the FRESH current args (restore).
- Result: mpv host stuck at maximize geometry (3840x2137) while main window is restored (1934x2167). Visible seam where host extends beyond WebView.

Fix: in sync_geometry's non-throttled path, always apply the CURRENT call args. Clear pending (discard it). Rationale: every Resized event triggers a fresh sync_geometry, so the latest call already has the freshest data; pending exists only as a 'last drag position never lost' safety net, but the next event already covers that case. Worst case after fix: a drag that ends within the 50ms throttle window may miss the final ~50ms of motion, which is imperceptible. Likely supersedes prexu-cjo.

rust, prexu, anti-fabrication

title: [bug] mini-player: video host not topmost, other apps cover the video
description: From 2026-05-09 testing (correction to prexu-ltu issue #2): when mini-player is active, the Tauri WebView overlay floats above other apps thanks to set_always_on_top(true) on the main window, BUT the mpv video host stays in the regular z-order so other apps render BETWEEN the always-on-top overlay and the video underneath. User saw the player chrome / time / next-episode controls floating above their screenshot tool, but the actual video was hidden behind it. Root cause: HostWindow is a WS_POPUP sibling top-level window (host_window.rs:7); set_always_on_top on the Tauri WebViewWindow only flips the topmost flag for that HWND, not the sibling host. Fix: added HostWindow::set_topmost(bool) using SetWindowPos with HWND_TOPMOST/HWND_NOTOPMOST + a PlayerState::apply_host_topmost wrapper that also re-anchors below the main window in the topmost group. enter_mini calls apply_host_topmost(true) after the main set_always_on_top + geometry; exit_mini calls apply_host_topmost(false). Now both windows are topmost together with the WebView overlaying the video, and other apps cannot cover either one.

description: From 2026-05-09 testing: closing the player while in mini mode left the entire Tauri main window at 480x270 with always-on-top still set. Root cause: handleExit in Player.tsx called player_unload directly, which only tears down mpv — it never restored the pre-mini outer geometry stashed by player_enter_mini. Fix: handleExit now checks mini.isMini and calls mini.toggleMini() (which dispatches playerExitMini) BEFORE invoking player_unload, so the main window is restored to its pre-mini outer geometry and always-on-top is cleared on every exit path (back button, ESC, EOF auto-exit).

title: True floating mini-player: keep main app navigable during playback
description: UX feedback from prexu-ltu testing (2026-05-09): user expected mini-player to behave like Plex's 'minimize' — main app stays at full size and remains navigable (browse Library, check cast/crew, run scans, etc.) while a small floating video window keeps playing in a corner. Current implementation (prexu-a6z.1 + .2 MVP) shrinks the WHOLE Tauri window to 480x270, so the rest of the app is hidden until the user exits mini mode. This is a meaningful redesign of mini-mode: leave the main window at its previous size, render the player chrome only over the small region that overlays the host, and let React Router show whatever route the user navigates to elsewhere in the WebView. Architectural notes: the mpv HostWindow is already a sibling Win32 window of the WebView, so it CAN persist across React route changes — the surrounding app would just need a floating mini chrome component + a global PlayerContext flag for 'in-floating-mode' so the rest of the app knows playback is live. Restore-to-full-player button + persisted geometry per-monitor + click-through interaction zones are the main design points. Substantial scope — own session.

title: [bug] alphabet scrubber lands short and below target row
description: Two related symptoms when clicking a letter higher in the alphabet (e.g. 'D' from a viewport showing 'A-C'): (1) the first click scrolls but doesn't reach the target letter's first row — that row is below the viewport. A second click scrolls further but only just barely shows the target row at the bottom. (2) The user expects the target row at the TOP of the viewport, not the bottom. Root cause: VirtualizedLibraryGrid's scrollToIndex was calling tanstack/react-virtual's virtualizer.scrollToIndex with align:'center' + behavior:'smooth'. For long alphabet jumps the virtualizer estimates row sizes for unmeasured rows; cumulative estimate error puts the smooth scroll at the wrong offset. Once the destination window renders, measureElement updates the cache but the smooth scroll has already settled. Fix: changed to align:'start', behavior:'auto', and added two RAF settle passes that re-issue scrollToIndex once the just-rendered rows have been measured. Surfaced 2026-05-09 by Nick during library testing on a ~500-show library.
close_reason: Fixed in src/components/VirtualizedLibraryGrid.tsx scrollToIndex: switched align:'center' → 'start' (target row at top of viewport, matching user expectation for alphabet jumps) and behavior:'smooth' → 'auto' (instant). Added a two-RAF settle loop that re-issues scrollToIndex on the next animation frame and again the frame after — by then the just-revealed rows have been measured by the virtualizer's measureElement callback, so the corrected (rather than estimated) cumulative offset gets applied. Non-virtualized fallback also switched to block:'start' for consistency. tsc + vitest 1232/1232 clean. Browser verification still required (the bug only reproduces on a virtualized library with hundreds of rows + a long alphabet jump).

On the native (mpv) path videoRef.current is null because there's no <video> element (mpv renders into a sibling Win32 HostWindow), so the call silently returned without any logger output or invocation. Fixed by adding useMiniPlayer hook (calls playerEnterMini/playerExitMini from prexu-a6z.1) and branching by IS_NATIVE_PLAYER in Player.tsx so the same button drives the native mini-player on Tauri and the browser PiP everywhere else. On native (mpv) the button now invokes the Win32 mini-player; on HTML5 the existing browser PiP is unchanged. title: Player as overlay (not route) — instant exit + page persistence
description:
**Goal**: clicking Stop returns the user to the page they were on with **no perceptible gap** — matching Plex's UX. Today, Player is a peer route to AppLayout (App.tsx:200-219), so stopping triggers a full AppLayout + destination route remount (~1s in dev, prexu-zq4). The fix is architectural: keep AppLayout always mounted, render Player as a top-level overlay driven by context, never unmount the underlying page when entering playback.

User-confirmed scope (2026-05-04):
- OK to lose: URL-based deep-linking into /play/X, browser back/forward toggling player open/closed, bookmarks of /play/X.
- Must keep: in-player Prev/Next episode for shows + playlists (currently navigate(`/play/${next}`)).
- Defer: Watch Together URL-based entry (separate WT refactor later — single-user UX is the priority).

## Architecture

**New: src/contexts/PlayerContext.tsx**
- State: `{ playing: { ratingKey: string; offset?: number; session?: WTOptions } | null }`
- Methods: `play(ratingKey, options?)`, `stop()`, `replaceRatingKey(nextKey)` (for in-player prev/next without unmount/remount churn)
- Provider mounted at App.tsx top level under AppProviders

**Modified: src/App.tsx**
- Remove the standalone `<Route path="/play/:ratingKey" element={...} />` route
- Render `<PlayerOverlay />` at top level (sibling to <Routes>) — conditionally renders Player when context.playing != null
- Keep a thin `/play/:ratingKey` bridge route inside AppLayout that, on mount, reads URL params, calls context.play(...), then navigates(replace) to lastNonPlayerRoute. This is what lets WT invite URLs and external deep-links still work without resurrecting the standalone route.

**Modified: src/pages/Player.tsx**
- handleExit → context.stop() (no navigate)
- handleNextEpisode / handlePrevEpisode → context.replaceRatingKey() (in-place ratingKey swap; existing usePlayer ratingKey effect re-inits)
- Remove sessionStorage lastNonPlayerRoute machinery (not needed when AppLayout never unmounts)

**Modified call sites (15 files)** — replace navigate(`/play/${k}`) with context.play(k):
- src/hooks/usePlayAction.tsx (4 sites — main play action helper)
- src/pages/Dashboard.tsx (3)
- src/pages/PlaylistDetail.tsx (2)
- src/pages/CollectionDetail.tsx (2)
- src/pages/ItemDetail.tsx (1)
- src/pages/Downloads.tsx (1)
- src/components/detail/ItemHeroSection.tsx (6)
- src/components/player/QueuePanel.tsx (1)
- src/components/InviteNotification.tsx (1) — WT, can stay as URL nav for now via the bridge route
- src/components/SessionCreator.tsx (1) — WT, same
- src/hooks/watch-together/useRemoteMediaSwitch.ts (1) — WT, same
- src/hooks/watch-together/useWatchTogetherSession.ts (1) — WT, same

**Test updates**:
- src/hooks/usePlayAction.test.tsx — mock PlayerContext instead of useNavigate
- src/components/InviteNotification.test.tsx — keep existing nav-based assertion (bridge route)
- App-level tests if any assume a /play route

## Cleanup once landed

- Remove the route-transition spinner in AppLayout (shipped 3306cff for prexu-zq4) — no longer needed because there's no transition gap.
- Re-evaluate Player.tsx body-bg manipulation — with AppLayout always mounted under the player, the body navy paint may also be removable. Verify Discord/desktop bleed isn't reintroduced (per the native-player-fullscreen memory).

## Acceptance

1. **Stop click**: time from click to underlying page being interactive is one frame (<16ms perceived). No spinner, no navy fill, no remount of AppLayout/destination page.
2. **In-player Prev/Next**: switching episodes within a show or playlist works as today (player chrome stays, content swaps). No AppLayout remount, no navigation.
3. **WT URLs**: clicking a WT invite link (which contains /play/X?session=...) still launches playback with the session active. Single-user playback no longer goes through any /play/* URL.
4. **Tests**: full vitest suite green; tsc clean.
5. **Manual**: dev tauri build — Stop on movie/episode is visually instant; library/dashboard/item-detail page is exactly as it was when Play was clicked (scroll position preserved via existing useScrollRestoration).

## Out of scope (file separately if needed)
- Player URL deep-linking
- Browser back/forward integration with player open/closed
- WT entry refactor (single-user is the priority — WT keeps working via the bridge route)

title: Stop click: navy exit overlay visible for ~2s before navigate
description:
Symptom (2026-05-03 user report on dev tauri build): clicking Stop on the native player paints the navy exitOverlay (introduced by prexu-cuq) and leaves it visible for about 2 seconds before the previous page renders.

Root cause: src-tauri/src/player/mod.rs destroy() did a synchronous bounded-join on the mpv event pump with a 2s timeout (recv_timeout(Duration::from_secs(2))). The TS handleExit awaits player_unload, so when the join hits its timeout (often, in practice — mpv's wait_event(1.0) only breaks on the NEXT iteration after Shutdown), the React UI is held up for the full 2s. The exit overlay sits on screen for that whole window.

Fix: split destroy() into a synchronous silence phase (mute, pause, queue stop, queue quit) and a background teardown phase (pump join, host drop, terminate_destroy on Inner drop). The Tauri command returns in <50ms after silence; the slow teardown happens on a spawned thread without blocking the user. Audio is already cut by the synchronous mute, so the deferred work has no user-visible cost.

## Required Skills
rust,tauri,prexu,anti-fabrication
close_reason: destroy() now returns synchronously after mute+pause+stop+quit; pump join + host drop + terminate moved to a background thread so handleExit's await resolves in <50ms instead of up to 2s.

title: Next Episode prompt fires on movies due to stale queue + missing content-type gate
description:
**Symptom (verified)**: Played 10 Cloverfield Lane (a movie). Near end credits, a 'Next Episode' button appeared. Clicked it — went to Yu Yu Hakusho S01E02, which had been the test content from a prior day's session.

**Root cause** (found): src/pages/Player.tsx:335-337:
```ts
const hasNextItem =
  queue.currentIndex + 1 < queue.items.length ||
  episodeNav.handleNextEpisode != null;
```

For a movie, episodeNav.handleNextEpisode is null (correct — Plex movies don't have episode siblings). But queue.items still contains yesterday's Yu Yu Hakusho episode because src/contexts/QueueContext.tsx:48 persists the queue to localStorage and never clears it. So hasNextItem becomes true via the queue-fallback branch, useSkipSegments synthesizes the 'Next Episode' segment (commit 8e42202), and clicking it advances the queue to whatever's there.

**Two distinct issues, one user impact**:

1. **Synthesis doesn't gate by content type.** A 'Next Episode' label is meaningless for a movie. Even with a populated queue, clicking it shouldn't say 'Next Episode' for a movie. Either:
   - Gate hasNextItem synthesis to player.itemType === 'episode' (current item is an episode, so 'next' makes sense), OR
   - Rename the synthesized prompt to 'Up Next' / 'Play Next' for non-episode contexts, OR
   - Don't synthesize at all when current item isn't an episode.

2. **Queue persists stale items across app sessions.** Even if (1) is fixed for movies, a queued YYH ep from yesterday surviving into today is questionable UX. Options:
   - Clear queue on app launch (aggressive — loses 'oh I queued these last night, let me resume').
   - Add per-item timestamps + TTL (e.g. 24h).
   - Clear queue on Stop / when leaving the player without auto-advancing.
   - Leave as-is and rely on (1) to gate the prompt — accept that the queue may contain unrelated content but only surface it when the *current* content matches its type.

Recommend doing (1) first — minimal risk, fixes the user-visible bug directly. Decide on (2) afterward as separate UX policy.

**Acceptance**: While playing a movie, no 'Next Episode' button appears, even with non-empty queue. While playing an episode with a queued unrelated item (e.g. movie), behavior is intentional per the (2) decision.

## Required Skills
react,prexu
notes: Reproduced 2026-05-03 by user after testing prexu-aua. ratingKey for 10 Cloverfield Lane was 26800; clicking Next Episode took user to YYH S01E02. Queue persistence file: src/contexts/QueueContext.tsx:48.
close_reason:
Fixed in src/pages/Player.tsx by introducing a single 'logical next' notion (hasNextItem = itemType==='episode' AND (queue has next OR Plex episode-nav has next)) and threading it to all three sites that previously had per-call-site gates that didn't agree:
1. useSkipSegments hasNextEpisode arg — was already gated indirectly via hasNextItem prior to this change.
2. SkipSegmentButton hasNextEpisode prop — was '!!handleNextEpisode' (always true). Now uses hasNextItem.
3. PostPlayScreen useEffect trigger — was 'remainingCount > 0' (any queue items). Now uses hasNextItem.

Verified by user on dev: movies show 'Skip Credits' (not 'Next Episode') and don't pop PostPlay at end. Episodes still get 'Next Episode' + PostPlayScreen at credits/EOF (regression-free).

title: Audio leaks 2-3s after Stop — destroy is async to React unmount
description:
**Symptom**: Clicking Stop in the player. Video disappears immediately, dashboard renders (background painted), but audio continues playing for ~2-3 seconds before fully cutting out.

**Reproduced** on installed Prexu_0.6.3 (commit 09054f0) playing ratingKey=63168 with audio enhancements preset=night. Logs show full destroy() sequence completes within the same wall-clock second (mute → pause → stop → quit → pump joined in 18ms → host dropped at 28ms total). So the leak isn't from a slow Rust-side teardown.

**Root cause**: src/hooks/player/useNativePlayer.ts:382 fires `invoke("player_unload")` from a useEffect cleanup. useEffect cleanups CANNOT be async — returning a Promise from one is a no-op for React. So when the player unmounts:
1. React cleanup runs, fires `invoke("player_unload")` and immediately returns
2. React continues unmounting the player overlay → navigates back → dashboard renders → user sees background paint
3. *Concurrently*, Tauri command thread is still inside Player::destroy() doing mute/pause/stop/quit/join/mpv_terminate_destroy
4. mpv's audio-buffer (~200ms decoded-ahead) + WASAPI's already-submitted samples continue playing during steps 2-3

The historical 'fix' captured in the superseded memory ('destroy() joins event pump before dropping Inner so mpv_terminate_destroy runs sync and silences audio') is correct on the Rust side but doesn't help — the Rust side runs concurrently with React's unmount, not before it.

**Fix candidates** (not decided):
1. **React side**: Move player_unload OUT of useEffect cleanup. Have the Stop button (and PostPlayScreen Stop, and back-nav) AWAIT `invoke("player_unload")` before navigating. Component then unmounts after mpv is already terminated. Simplest fix; won't catch unmount-via-tab-close paths.
2. **Rust side**: Detach WASAPI before stop. Set `ao=null` (or `command ["ao_reload"]`) at the start of destroy() to immediately route mpv audio to a null sink, killing the OS audio session within mpv's tick rather than waiting for buffered samples to drain. Keeps the React side simple but adds a libmpv2 audio API touchpoint.
3. **Both**: 1 for the fast path, 2 as backstop for unmount-via-other-paths.

Recommend evaluating fix 1 first — small, contained to useNativePlayer.ts, and explains the user-observed race directly.

**Acceptance**: After Stop, audio cuts within 200ms of video disappearing (or simultaneously). Reproduce by playing 30s into any file then clicking Stop.

## Required Skills
react,rust,prexu,anti-fabrication
notes: Surfaced during prexu-2zo.3 retest 2026-05-03. Smoke test still soft-passing on direct play but this is a clear UX regression vs the SUPERSEDED 'fullscreen-host-window-geometry-sync-confirmed' memory which claimed destroy was synchronous end-to-end.
close_reason: Fixed in src/pages/Player.tsx handleExit: await invoke('player_unload') BEFORE navigate(target). The useEffect cleanup that ran after unmount was firing player_unload fire-and-forget, racing mpv terminate against React's next paint and leaving 2-3s of buffered audio playing while the dashboard was already showing. Awaiting in handleExit pushes the route change until destroy() returns; the cleanup then no-ops via 'destroy: nothing to destroy'. User-verified: 'audio is correctly cut off on stop'.

title: Native player: 30s first-play cold start on installed build
description:
**Symptom**: First HEVC playback after a fresh install takes ~30 seconds before first frame is presented. Subsequent plays (after closing/reopening Prexu) start in <1 second. Reproduced on installed Prexu_0.6.3_x64-setup.exe at C:\Users\grand\AppData\Local\Prexu\prexu.exe.

**Diagnostic data** (from %LOCALAPPDATA%\com.prexu.client\logs\Prexu.log, UTC):

First play (cold):
```
23:19:01  load_url offset=94339ms
23:19:01  ensure_init OK, HostWindow created, loadfile sent
   ⏱ 12 second gap with NO log entries from mpv
23:19:13  apply_sub_style fires (React side reacted to something — likely FileLoaded that we didn't observe in this session at our log level)
```

Second play of a different file (warm), after closing/reopening Prexu:
```
23:26:27  load_url offset=120500ms
23:26:27  ensure_init OK, HostWindow created, mpv created, loadfile sent
23:26:27  FileLoaded → player://ready    ← all in same wall-clock second
```

**Likely contributors** (from highest to lowest confidence):
1. Windows Defender / AV first-execution scan of the unsigned Prexu.exe (19MB) and libmpv-2.dll (117MB). Plausible because second run is unaffected.
2. Cold mpv hwdec probing (auto-safe → d3d11va selection on Windows).
3. Cold Plex direct-streaming connection — first byte-range request to plex.direct on a fresh run can stall.

**Possible mitigations to evaluate** (no decision yet):
- Code-sign the installer/binary (prevents some AV scan delays; needs signing cert).
- Warm-init mpv at app launch (create a dummy player + libmpv handle so the DLL is paged in and hwdec is probed before user clicks play). Trade-off: small idle CPU/memory cost.
- 'Preparing playback…' UI affordance during the cold-load phase so the user knows we're working.
- Investigate whether the 12s gap is mpv side or Plex side by adding more granular logging (track when first byte-range response arrives).

**Acceptance**: First-play time-to-first-frame on a fresh install drops to ≤5s OR an unambiguous loading affordance is shown during the cold load. Whichever lands first.

title: Episode list: hover thumbnail to play, click text to view detail
description:
On the season episode list (e.g. /item/<show>/season/<n> — see screenshot of Yu Yu Hakusho 'Spirit Detective Saga' S1), each episode row has a thumbnail on the left and metadata (episode #, date, title, description) on the right. Currently the entire row is one click target that goes to the episode detail page.

Proposed:
- **Hover the THUMBNAIL** → reveals a play overlay (similar to dashboard cards). Clicking the thumbnail starts playback directly via /play/<episodeKey>.
- **Click the right-side text region** (episode #, date, title, description) → unchanged behaviour: navigates to the episode detail page (/item/<episodeKey>).

This makes binge-watching dramatically faster — no detail page round-trip — while keeping the existing per-episode info path for users who want to read details first.

Considerations:
- Hover-to-play on TV/remote doesn't translate; keyboard focus on the thumbnail should also reveal the play affordance, and Enter on a focused thumbnail starts playback.
- For mobile/touch: tap on thumbnail = play, tap on text = detail (no hover needed).
- Should respect viewOffset (resume vs from-start) — same logic as the existing play buttons on detail pages.

Acceptance:
- Hovering an episode's thumbnail on desktop reveals a play button overlay.
- Clicking the play overlay (or the thumbnail) navigates to /play/<ratingKey> with the saved viewOffset.
- Clicking anywhere in the right-side text column navigates to /item/<ratingKey> as today.
- Keyboard focus on the thumbnail also shows the play affordance and Enter triggers playback.

title: Library alphabet scrubber: clicking does nothing + needs to be larger
description:

1. **Clicks are non-functional.** Clicking any letter does not jump the grid to items starting with that letter. Either the click handler isn't wired, the scroll-to logic doesn't find a matching item, or the scrubber is non-interactive entirely.

title: Re-enable autoplay UX flow after Stop/disable
description:
Once a user clicks 'Stop' on PostPlayScreen or unchecks the AUTO PLAY ON checkbox, there's no UI surface to re-enable autoplay for subsequent episodes' PostPlay overlays. autoplayEnabled is local React state in PostPlayScreen, so it resets to true when the overlay re-mounts — but if EOF detection breaks (or the user navigates via SkipSegmentButton's Next Episode instead of letting EOF fire), they may go many episodes without seeing the PostPlay overlay at all and lose track of the setting.

Surfaced during Phase 3 dev verification: user lost the autoplay flow entirely once SkipSegmentButton-driven navigation took over.

Possible fixes:
- Persist autoplay-on preference in playback prefs (alongside skipIntroEnabled / skipCreditsEnabled).
- Add a small autoplay toggle in PlayerControls or in a settings flyout so it's always reachable.
- Show the PostPlay overlay's autoplay toggle even when the next-episode jump came from SkipSegmentButton (would require triggering PostPlay on user-initiated nav, which is a different UX).

title: Add Stop button in player controls; make Back navigate history
description:
The current back-arrow in PlayerControls performs two roles:
- Exit the player (return to library)
- Implicitly halt playback (player_unload runs in cleanup)

After clicking Next Episode (or letting autoplay advance), clicking Back returns to the PREVIOUS episode (browser history pop) instead of exiting the player. From the user's perspective there's no obvious way to stop watching short of using ESC or pressing Back multiple times.

Proposal:
- Replace (or augment) the back arrow with an explicit Stop button that quits the player session entirely (navigate to /library or to the last non-/play/* route).
- Or keep both: Back = navigate(-1) (history pop), Stop = navigate to safe non-player route.
- Stop should also dispatch player_unload eagerly so audio stops immediately even if the next route's render is slow.

Discussion needed on:
- Which icon / position
- Whether to map Stop to a keyboard shortcut (Q? Shift+Esc?)
- TV-app future: a remote 'Back' button typically maps to history-pop; we need a separate 'Exit' or 'Home' affordance for the player

Could be combined with prexu-w0j (Skip Credits / Next Episode separation) since both are player-control UX rethinks.

Surfaced during Phase 3 dev verification.

Exit calls a new handler that reads sessionStorage 'prexu.lastNonPlayerRoute' (written by App.tsx on every non-/play route change) so the user lands on the page they pressed Play from rather than walking back through every auto-advanced episode in history. title: ErrorBoundary: Cannot read properties of null (reading '1') during HMR
description:
During an active dev session with Vite HMR firing on backend pushes, the React ErrorBoundary fallback briefly displayed 'Something went wrong / Cannot read properties of null (reading '1')'. Playback resumed afterward. Likely a transient HMR state where one module reloaded before another caught up.

Reproducer attempt: difficult — only seen during heavy edit-save cycles in dev. Production builds shouldn't hit this since modules aren't replaced live.

Triage path:
