title: Skip segment dismiss persists across episodes
description:
When the user clicks the 'x' to dismiss the SkipSegmentButton (intro/credits/Next Episode prompt), the dismissal silently persists into subsequent episodes — the button never reappears.

Two compounding causes in useSkipSegments.ts:
1. useSegments cache key is markers.length + chapters.length only (line 106-110). Most TV episodes have 2 markers (intro + credits), so the cache returns the OLD episode's segment objects with their old startMs values. The dismissedRef set keys on `${type}-${startMs}` and matches the stale entries — segments get filtered out before reaching state.
2. dismissedRef (line 37) is a per-hook-instance useRef with no reset. Player.tsx stays mounted across React Router navigations, so the Set lives forever for the session.

Fix: switch useSegments cache to reference equality on markers/chapters arrays (Plex returns fresh arrays per metadata fetch, so === changes between episodes). Reset dismissedRef in a useEffect when markers/chapters references change.

## Required Skills
react,prexu
close_reason: Fixed in commit ff149b3. useSegments cache now keys on markers/chapters reference identity (was length-only, which collided across same-shape episodes). Added a useEffect in useSkipSegments that clears dismissedRef + activeSegment when markers or chapters references change — Player.tsx stays mounted across React Router navigations so the Set otherwise persisted for the entire session. Existing 9 useSkipSegments tests pass; full vitest suite 1163/1163 pass.

title: PostPlay: Stop button doesn't stop playback when autoplay is still on
description:
Clicking Stop on PostPlayScreen while 'AUTO PLAY ON' is checked closes the overlay but playback restarts (the next episode keeps loading/playing). Workaround: uncheck AUTO PLAY ON first, then click Stop — that actually halts.

Likely cause: handlePostPlayStop in Player.tsx:243 only does setShowPostPlay(false). It doesn't:
- Cancel the PostPlayScreen autoplay-on-zero countdown interval
- Pause/unload the player

So the autoplay countdown keeps ticking even after the overlay is hidden, eventually firing onPlayNext via the unmount cleanup or interval racing the unmount. Or a second EOF-reaction handler runs because the underlying player wasn't paused.

Likely fix:
- onStop in PostPlayScreen should ALSO clear the autoplay interval (mirror handleToggleAutoPlay's clearInterval branch).
- handlePostPlayStop should also explicitly pause the player or call player_unload to stop the underlying playback (which also resolves bug #1's underlying-playback-progressing concern).

Acceptance: with AUTO PLAY ON checked, clicking Stop closes the overlay AND halts playback (no further loading, no audio bleed-through). Same behavior as clicking Stop with autoplay off.

Related to prexu-postplay-autoplay (filed alongside) — probably fixable in one change.

## Required Skills
react,prexu
close_reason: Root cause was the event pump emitting player://eof for every libmpv EndFile event regardless of reason. STOP/QUIT (which fire when player_unload tears down mpv during episode-change navigation) were misinterpreted as natural EOF, so when the user clicked the SkipSegmentButton's 'Next Episode' label (mid-credits), the OLD player's STOP fired player://eof, React showed PostPlay for the queue position AFTER the just-loaded next ep, and the audio/video underneath was the legitimately-loaded new episode. Fixed in events.rs by filtering EndFile to reason==0 (EOF only). Player.tsx also got defensive player_pause dispatches on PostPlay open and Stop click as belt-and-suspenders for any future regression. cargo check + tsc clean.

title: Tighten release.yml cleanup tag pattern

title: Step 6.4 — code-signing impact assessment
description:
libmpv-2.dll is third-party — does Windows SmartScreen flag it inside the bundle? Does existing code-signing path cover bundled resources or do we need to also sign the DLL? Document findings.

## Required Skills
github,security,documentation
close_reason: Documented findings in docs/code-signing.md. Key conclusions: (1) No Authenticode signing exists today — TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY is for the updater (minisign), not signtool. (2) SmartScreen does not inspect bundled DLLs; warnings are caused by the unsigned installer/.exe themselves and would exist with or without libmpv. (3) Once we add Authenticode signing, also sign libmpv-2.dll (in CI before tauri-action runs, so the build.rs copy carries the signature into the bundle) — large unsigned third-party DLLs occasionally trip AV heuristics. (4) Recommended workflow change documented (signCommand in tauri.conf.json, EV cert via thumbprint, mandatory /tr timestamp). (5) Code signing is separate tech debt — not blocking the Phase 6 ship.

title: [cleanup] Strip iteration-history + beads-ticket comments from player code (CLAUDE.md compliance sweep)
description:
CLAUDE.md / core comment-discipline rule: 'Don't reference the current task, fix, or callers ("used by X", "added for the Y flow", "handles the case from issue #123"), since those belong in the PR description and rot as the codebase evolves.'

The native-player branch accumulated a lot of inline beads-ticket references and 'previously this did X' narrative as the developer iterated through hard Win32 transparency bugs. The current state is correct but the comments document HOW we got here, not WHY the current invariant holds.

Examples (non-exhaustive):
- Player.tsx L116 — references ensure_init + pb.subtitleStyle + the load case + prexu-id
- Player.tsx L131-141 — five-issue archaeology of mask-based hiding (prexu-s0f / 4ml / ya6 etc.)
- Player.tsx L226-229 — '7il.4: the native path NOW has a SECOND button…'
- Player.tsx L432-447 — handleExitRef rationale referencing prexu-jk0
- AppLayout.tsx L120-128 — 5-step history of hiding mechanism (kfa → ya6 → cay → 9bk → quq)
- src-tauri/src/player/mod.rs — extensive (prexu-buw), (prexu-hhx), (prexu-7il.2 / 7il.7), (prexu-ifj), (prexu-bm0) annotations
- src-tauri/src/player/commands.rs — same density

A reader six months from now does not care which ticket introduced a behavior — git blame answers that. They care about the structural invariant the code is enforcing NOW.

Proposed:
- Sweep all of src/pages/Player.tsx, src/components/AppLayout.tsx, src/components/player/MiniChrome.tsx, src/contexts/PlayerContext.tsx, src-tauri/src/player/*.rs, src/hooks/player/useNativePlayer.ts
- Delete: all (prexu-NNN) parentheticals, all 'previously…' / 'this was changed because…' narrative, all 'NOW does X' temporal language.
- Keep: structural invariants ('Win32 windows are thread-affine, so SetWindowPos from a non-owner thread blocks indefinitely'), non-obvious WHYs ('flushSync because the Dashboard commit takes ~1s and would leak transparent pixels').
- Target ≥ 200 LOC of comment deletion with zero loss of structural understanding.

Acceptance:
- 0 'prexu-' references in src/ and src-tauri/ comments
- 0 'NOW' / 'previously' / 'used to' / 'we now' in comments
- Spot-check: any retained comment, removed in isolation, would leave a future reader confused about an invariant

This is a single mechanical sweep; do it AFTER the structural extractions (prexu-ps6, prexu-ve9, prexu-m1g) land so we're not stripping comments out of code that's about to move anyway.

title: Cold-start: warm libmpv handle at app launch to eliminate 12s hwdec probe
description:
Concrete trace captured 2026-05-23 02:09:12 (fresh-install run after cargo build). prexu-ps1's diagnostic logs landed at info, so we now have the per-stage timing:

- 02:09:12.x: load_url → mpv init → loadfile sent
- 02:09:34: FileLoaded → player://ready (+22s — cold plex.direct TLS handshake + demuxer parses moov)
- 02:09:46: playback-restart, hwdec-current=d3d11va, time-pos starts moving (+12s — hardware decoder probe + first frame decode)
- Total cold→first-frame: 34s

Dominant: network/demuxer (22s). Secondary: hwdec probe (12s).

The 12s gap is what the user sees as 'stuck at 00:00:00' after the loading screen dismisses — FileLoaded is what dismisses the loading screen, but actual playback waits on hwdec.

Proposed first attempt (from prexu-ps1 closure): warm-init. In the Tauri setup hook, spawn a hidden libmpv handle, set hwdec=auto-safe + vo=gpu-next, run a no-op probe (load a tiny built-in test pattern or ), then keep the handle alive. When user clicks Play, the existing ensure_init reuses the warmed handle; hwdec is already chosen and the D3D11 device is initialised. Expected gain: most of the 12s gap.

Network dominator (22s) is harder — TLS handshake + first byte-range from Plex direct cannot be pre-warmed without knowing the URL. Two paths: (a) DNS pre-resolve to plex.direct on app launch (gets you ~100ms of the 22s), (b) keep an idle keep-alive HTTPS connection to the user's PMS so the TLS session is resumable. Both are speculative — instrument first to confirm where in the 22s the time actually goes (TLS handshake vs byte-range latency vs moov parse).

Acceptance: measured cold→first-frame drops below 15s on a fresh-install run, with the timing logs landed in prexu-ps1 used as the canonical attribution method.

title: [cleanup] make mpv host a child of Tauri main window so drag is free; current sync_geometry throttle visibly lags
description:
Surfaced 2026-05-21 from pop-out drag testing (image #41). Today the mpv host is a sibling Win32 window of the WebView, repositioned on each Tauri WindowEvent::Resized/Moved via sync_geometry (50ms throttle). During an active drag Windows fires hundreds of move events but most are dropped by the throttle; mpv visibly lags behind the popout window during drag and sometimes ends up partially outside the new window position.

Image #42 shows the symptom: mpv stuck on a single frame for several seconds because a critical sync_geometry call was dropped.

Architectural fix: make mpv host a CHILD of the Tauri main window (not a sibling). Win32 child windows automatically follow their parent's translation — no event-driven sync needed for pure drag. Resize still needs sync_geometry but only fires on actual resize, not on every move.

Transparency compositing should still work: mpv as a child behind the WebView2 (z-order child position), WebView2 still has transparent pixels in the mpv area, OS composites through.

Bigger architectural change than the context split. Needs careful testing on hwnd parenting, mpv's d3d11 swapchain behaviour, fullscreen transitions (prexu-dsh already noted dont resize mpv from host wndproc).

title: Dashboard/library: per-item incremental rendering instead of whole-shelf skeletons
description:
Better UX inspired by the official Plex client. Currently App.tsx's pre-fetch in AppRoutes() awaits getRecentlyAddedBySection for each section as a batch via Promise.all-style and only writes to the dashboard cache when the whole batch resolves; the dashboard shelf shows a unified row-level skeleton until all items arrive. The official Plex client renders items incrementally — each tile is its own loading state and pops in as it streams.

Surfaced 2026-05-21 by user during prexu-7il testing. Per-item rendering would noticeably improve perceived speed on first load and slow networks (e.g., remote Plex).

Refactor sketch:
- Each dashboard shelf component owns its own items array (state) and renders tiles individually with per-tile skeletons.
- Backing fetch produces items as they resolve (Promise.allSettled per item OR streaming-style array growth) rather than blocking on the whole batch.
- Consider migrating shelf data to React 19 use() + Suspense boundaries per row for cleaner UX integration.
- Apply same approach to /library/:sectionId LibraryView for scroll-down-before-loaded responsiveness.

Scope: dashboard shelves + LibraryView. Out of scope: detail pages, search.

title: Post-Player-unmount: AppLayout's empty Outlet shows body navy until destination paints
description:
Symptom (2026-05-03 user report): after clicking Stop, user sees a 1-2 second navy paint before the destination page renders. Confirmed via dev log:

- handleExit invokes player_unload — returns in 8ms (per c70a7d0 fix).
- Player cleanup useEffect runs ~1s later, judging by the 'cleanup: stopping timeline, unloading' log timestamp.
- Total navy duration matches user perception of 1-2s.

Root cause: src/components/AppLayout.tsx:178-183 has no background on the container — it's a flex column with header (var(--bg-secondary)) + sidebar + Outlet area. The Outlet area inherits the body bg (which Player.tsx's useLayoutEffect cleanup paints navy on unmount, per the 'native-player-fullscreen-host-window-geometry-sync-confirmed' memory). Until the destination route's lazy() chunk resolves AND the component renders content into the Outlet, the user sees navy from body bg.

Lazy routes (App.tsx:14-31): Player, Dashboard, LibraryView, ItemDetail, etc. All wrapped in a single <Suspense fallback={<LoadingScreen />}>. Even when chunks are pre-loaded, React + React Router commit takes a noticeable moment in dev.

Possible fixes (not yet picked):
1. Give AppLayout's main/Outlet area an explicit background that matches body (--bg-primary), so the body navy is no longer 'visible' through it. Simplest, addresses symptom not cause.
2. Replace the body-bg dance entirely. Player.tsx sets body transparent on mount because mpv host window shows through; restore on unmount. Alternative: render an absolutely-positioned navy panel underneath Player that only Player toggles, leaving body bg alone.
3. Pre-paint a Suspense fallback in the Outlet so the user sees a 'returning to library' state during the transition.

Acceptance: clicking Stop on the player returns to the previous page within ~200ms of perceived navy fill, OR shows a clear loading state instead of static navy.

title: Native player: investigate root cause of 30s cold-start
description:
Follow-up to prexu-95b. The user-facing affordance shipped — now investigate why first-play stalls ~30s on a fresh install while subsequent plays start in <1s.

Diagnostic data from the original report (Prexu_0.6.3_x64-setup.exe):
- 23:19:01 load_url offset=94339ms; ensure_init OK; HostWindow created; loadfile sent
- 12-second gap with NO mpv log entries
- 23:19:13 apply_sub_style fires (React side reacted to FileLoaded)

Likely contributors (from prexu-95b):
1. Windows Defender first-execution scan of unsigned Prexu.exe (19MB) and libmpv-2.dll (117MB)
2. Cold mpv hwdec probing (auto-safe → d3d11va selection)
3. Cold Plex direct-streaming connection — first byte-range to plex.direct on a fresh run

Suggested next steps:
- Add granular logging in src-tauri/src/player/* to distinguish 'mpv probe time' from 'first byte arrived from plex.direct'
- Prototype warm-init: spawn a hidden libmpv handle at app launch (Tauri setup hook) so the DLL is paged in and hwdec probed before user clicks Play. Measure delta against current cold path.
- Evaluate code-signing as a separate cost/benefit (requires cert procurement)

Acceptance: identify which contributor dominates the 12s gap with measurements, then either (a) implement the lowest-cost mitigation, or (b) document why the affordance is the right long-term answer.

close_reason: Investigation done; first-pass mitigation deferred until measured. Added 3 timing log statements at info-level to attribute the 12s cold-start gap: mod.rs:'starting mpv init (hwdec=auto-safe)' before mpv handle construction, events.rs FileLoaded bumped to info ('file loaded' is per-conventions an info-level transition), and events.rs first-non-zero duration logged at info as a 'stream opened' marker. Next cold-start trace will show ms deltas between (a) loadfile sent, (b) mpv init started, (c) first duration parsed (network/demuxer time), (d) FileLoaded (file ready), (e) PlaybackRestart (decoder up). Once we have those numbers from a fresh-install run we can decide whether warm-init (if hwdec dominates) or DNS pre-resolve (if network dominates) is the right next move. title: Player chrome stays visible during await player_unload after Stop
description:
**Symptom**: After fixing prexu-aua (audio leak), clicking Stop now correctly cuts audio immediately, but the Player.tsx UI (toolbar, transport controls, progress bar with the user's last position) stays on screen for the duration of the await player_unload roundtrip. User sees a 'blank' screen — controls + dark area, no video — for ~50-100ms before navigation completes.

**Root cause**: The handleExit fix awaits player_unload before calling navigate(target). During that await, the Player component is still mounted, so its UI keeps rendering. mpv has been terminated so the host window's video is gone, but the React UI is unaware.

**Fix candidates**:
1. **Hide the Player UI immediately when handleExit starts.** Add a local 'isExiting' state set true at the top of handleExit; conditionally suppress the controls overlay and paint a solid black fill while exiting. User sees a clean cut to black, then navigation. Works without changing async flow.
2. **Optimistic navigate first, then unload.** navigate(target) happens immediately; player_unload runs concurrently. Defeats the prexu-aua audio fix, so do NOT do this.
3. **Loading 'Stopping…' overlay during the await.** Like option 1 but with a small spinner/text. Probably overkill for ~100ms.

Recommend option 1 — minimal, preserves audio fix.

**Acceptance**: From Stop click to dashboard render, no intermediate state where controls are visible against an empty video region. Either pure black or pure dashboard, no in-between.

description:
**Symptom**: While playing, controls + cursor fade after 3s of inactivity (correct). User clicks pause — controls and cursor stay visible (correct). User clicks unpause WITHOUT moving the mouse — controls and cursor remain visible indefinitely until the user moves the mouse and waits another 3s.

**Root cause**: src/hooks/player/usePlayerControlsVisibility.ts only schedules the hide timer in two places:

title: Native player: cursor doesn't hide on inactivity
description:
**Symptom**: While playing video on the native player (Windows installed build), the mouse cursor remains visible indefinitely instead of auto-hiding after inactivity. HTML5 player path correctly hides cursor with controls.

**Root cause**: src/pages/Player.tsx:480-484 explicitly disables cursor hiding on IS_NATIVE_PLAYER. Comment in that block says: 'On native path, never hide the cursor — WebView2 passes mouse events through transparent areas, so cursor: none + transparent webview = permanently lost cursor (onMouseMove can't fire to bring controls back).'

That tradeoff was a workaround for a real-or-theorized WebView2 quirk, but leaves a UX regression: cursor sits on top of fullscreen video forever.

**Fix paths to evaluate** (no decision yet):

1. **Validate the original concern empirically.** Try `cursor: none` on the native path and see if onMouseMove still fires on a higher container (document body, a non-transparent overlay). The fact that PlayerControls overlay reappears on mouse move on the HTML5 path suggests onMouseMove isn't actually broken — likely the comment author was being defensive. If it works, just delete the IS_NATIVE_PLAYER carve-out.

2. **Hide via OS API on the HostWindow** instead of CSS. Add a Tauri command (e.g. `player_set_host_cursor_visible(visible: bool)`) that calls Win32 SetCursor(NULL) / ShowCursor(FALSE) on the HostWindow. CSS cursor styling on the WebView is unaffected. This is the most surgical fix but adds another Win32 surface.

3. **Move the cursor-hide CSS to a non-transparent fullscreen-only overlay.** When playing, paint an opaque-but-invisible (alpha=0 with pointer-events:none) layer over the video region and apply cursor:none to it. Mouse events still bubble to whatever is below. This keeps WebView2 happy.

Recommend (1) first since it's a 1-line revert and the existing controls-visibility hook (usePlayerControlsVisibility) already handles mouse move tracking.

**Acceptance**: After ~3s of mouse inactivity over the player, cursor disappears. On any mouse movement, cursor reappears together with PlayerControls.

close_reason: Fixed by removing the IS_NATIVE_PLAYER carve-out on src/pages/Player.tsx:484. Original concern (cursor permanently lost on transparent WebView2) was a residual symptom from the fullscreen battle which has since been resolved (per the SUPERSEDED native-player-fullscreen-host-window-geometry-sync-confirmed memory). Tested 7 cases on the dev build — cursor hides after 3s of inactivity, reappears on mouse move, behaves correctly across fullscreen transitions and over the transparent video region. No more permanent-cursor-loss.

title: Move hwdec-current query from FileLoaded to first playback-restart
description:
**Symptom**: The hwdec-current logging added in commit 09054f0 returns PROPERTY_UNAVAILABLE (logged as '<unavailable>') because mpv's decoder hasn't initialized at FileLoaded time. From the 2026-05-03 16:40 installed-build run:

```
[player:events] hwdec-current=<unavailable> (file-loaded)
[player:events] video-codec=H.264 / AVC / MPEG-4 AVC / MPEG-4 part 10 video-format=h264
```

video-codec works fine since it's populated from the demuxer at file-load. hwdec-current is populated from the decoder which hasn't started yet.

**Fix**: Subscribe to mpv's playback-restart event (or observe video-out-params property) and query hwdec-current there instead of in FileLoaded. playback-restart fires after the first frame is decoded and presented — at that point hwdec-current reflects the actual selected backend.

Implementation note: Event::PlaybackRestart should be available from libmpv2; if not, observe property 'video-out-params' (it becomes available when the decoder is up).

**Acceptance**: After playing any file, log shows '[player:events] hwdec-current=d3d11va' (or similar real backend) at INFO level. blocks prexu-2zo.3 closure since 6.3 acceptance requires explicit hwdec engagement evidence.

title: Separate Skip Credits from Next Episode action
description:
SkipSegmentButton currently overloads the credits-time button: when a next episode exists, the label changes to 'Next Episode' and clicking navigates immediately. This conflates two distinct user intents:
- 'I want to skip past credits and watch what comes after them' (post-credits scenes are common in modern shows — Marvel-style stingers, anime omake, etc.)
- 'I'm done with this episode, take me to the next one'

Today the user can only do the second; there's no way to skip just the credits and stay in the current episode.

Design considerations:
- Two side-by-side buttons (Skip Credits | Next Episode) — works on desktop, awkward on TV remote (4-direction nav)
- Single button with a small split / dropdown — fiddly
- Default to Skip Credits, show Next Episode as a secondary action — symmetrical with intro behavior
- Default to Next Episode (current behavior) but add Shift+S or similar for Skip Credits only
- For TV/remote use case (mentioned by user as a future plan), single primary action + a secondary action with dedicated remote button is probably cleanest

Recommend two stacked buttons: primary = 'Skip Credits' (the literal name of the segment), secondary = 'Next Episode' if available. Both reachable by D-pad up/down on TV.

User context: surfaced when user expected the button labeled 'Next Episode' to do skipping behavior. They liked the split idea but flagged TV-app future as a concern.

title: Review audio normalization preset behavior on native player
description:
During Phase 3 dev verification (commit e5da260), the user confirmed the normalization preset switches (off / light / night) function — player_set_af_chain dispatches and mpv applies — but the audible behavior didn't match expectations.

Current Rust mapping (src-tauri/src/player/commands.rs player_set_af_chain):
- off:   af = ''
- light: af = 'lavfi=[loudnorm=I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11]'
- night: af = 'lavfi=[acompressor=threshold=-20dB:ratio=4:attack=5:release=50,loudnorm=I=-18]'

Compare against the HTML5 path (src/hooks/useAudioEnhancements.ts NORMALIZATION_PRESETS) which uses Web Audio DynamicsCompressorNode:
- light: threshold=-24, knee=30, ratio=4, attack=0.003, release=0.25
- night: threshold=-40, knee=10, ratio=12, attack=0.001, release=0.1

Two implementations diverge — light is much more aggressive on Web Audio than the loudnorm-based libmpv preset; night uses a heavier compressor on Web Audio but adds loudnorm on libmpv. Need to:

1. Define what each preset is supposed to *do* perceptually (e.g. light = gentle loudness normalization for varied content, night = strong dynamic compression for late-night low-volume listening).
2. Pick whichever implementation matches that intent and align the other to match.
3. Document the chosen filter graph in code comments.

May also want to expose finer controls (compressor knobs in expert mode) — out of scope for this task.

## Required Skills

title: Debounce/throttle player seek invocations during scrub drag
description: Log review from 2026-04-20 01:44:49-55 showed 5 seek commands fired within ~4 seconds, four of them to the identical target (199.77s). Every pointer movement on the seek bar invokes player_seek directly. Each seek triggers a full Plex transcode segment fetch + mpv seek + decode restart, so rapid drags stack up redundant work.\n\nFix: throttle seek to ~100-200ms during pointer-down, or fire only on pointer-up (with a preview display during drag). Preserve keyboard arrow-key behavior (each key press = one seek, no drag to debounce).
close_reason: Added throttle (leading + 150ms trailing) on drag-time seeks plus a guaranteed final seek on pointer-up; thumb tracks dragTime locally for instant visual feedback. 5 unit tests cover leading edge, throttle collapse, trailing flush, mouseup commit, and touch parity. Keyboard arrow seeks bypass this hook (per their own keybindings layer) so unchanged. vitest 1193/1193, tsc clean.

title: UX preference: also fade controls + cursor while paused
description:
**Current behavior**: While paused, controls + cursor are always visible (src/hooks/player/usePlayerControlsVisibility.ts useEffect on isPlaying — when isPlaying=false, set visible=true and clear timer).

**Proposed**: Fade controls + cursor after the same 3s inactivity timer even while paused. User would still get them back instantly on mouse move.

**Tradeoff**:
- Pro: cleaner view when user pauses to study a frame; cursor not obstructing the picture.
- Pro: behavior is symmetric whether playing or paused — simpler mental model.
- Con: less obvious that play/pause/seek are reachable. Common video-player convention (YouTube, Plex web) is 'show controls while paused' specifically because it signals 'we are waiting for you'.
- Con: might confuse new users who pause and don't realize they can move the mouse to get controls back.

Implementation is small — collapse the useEffect to one branch that always schedules the hide timer regardless of isPlaying. handleMouseMove + click handlers already restore visibility instantly.

**Acceptance**: While paused, after 3s of no mouse movement, cursor + controls fade. Mouse movement (or pressing any key bound to the player) restores them.
